item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was a down-vote for me. I do understand the concept and like it, as much as the Keter classification. But the text uses too much needless [REDACTED] for my taste, and looses itself in even more needless details, while some important facts should be more clear.

My impression is of a good and dangerous Skip, but with the documentation written almost as badly as the very -1 instances.

A limitation for the effects of -1 instances would also be good to improve verossimilitude. As it is, it is so contagious that I wonder only how it didn't ended the world already. So unstoppable that it is bland.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very well-written. Deserves compliment. The scip could have been much more complicated, handling objects related to liberal arts, yet you managed to give me a sort of creepiness elaborately hidden in nonsensical complicated vocabulary and sentences. Felt very elegant.

Have one complaint though - seems very containable for Keter object. I understand its potential danger but wouldn't Euclid be enough?

item.title: Staff Post - Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded: Version 10 of this article was reverted, as it changed 'amnesiacs' to 'amnestic'. While the change is technically correct, it is considered to be a stylistic choice for site-history reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In otherwords, upvote, needs work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I approve, but agree that it needs edits. I invite the author to try explaining Object-Oriented philosophy to a Heideggerian and a fan of Russell at the same time, and then to rewrite this. If they haven't succumbed to a real-life cognitohazard in the process, that is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very, very long without any payoff. It felt like it was building up to something, and when I realized it wasn't, it very quickly took away any interest I had.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think one of the biggest things about this that bugged is this. If I'm going to read a fictional nonsense philosophy, I want it to be an internally consistent and interesting fictional nonsense philosophy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've actually been reading some pretty dense material for my courses recently, and… this comes off as what someone utterly unacquainted with academics would think looks like academic writing.

Academic writing is not difficult because it uses a bunch of obscure words with each other in short order, it's difficult because it's using just a few terms you don't fully understand and combining them in very unintuitive ways. Hell, take Foucault as an example — in his works that I've seen, there are surprisingly few words that you wouldn't expect a high school graduate to know. The real meat, and the difficulty, lies in how he connects these ideas and draws conclusions from the information. I understand that you're deliberately not going to make something that actually does this, but it doesn't LOOK like it does this either. It doesn't hold my suspension of disbelief, really.

And beyond that, this just isn't terribly engaging. Maybe if you had chosen to give Sociosophy any sort of defining characteristics or themes that would make this more than Much Ado About Gibberish, but you didn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe it's because I majored in philosophy. I used to be surrounded by long, complex terms with densely packed definitions. But your terminology is haunting. The nonsense words you've pieced together are somehow creepy by themselves. As I worked out the pronunciation of each one, I felt as though I was naming something sinister.

I was completely in love with this SCP all through the containment procedures, and the description. I started to lose interest at the excerpts from the book. I feel like I didn't get a proper ending to the article.

Still, the good parts of this SCP are better than the entirety of many others SCPs. Upvoted for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The publishers take SCP-2233-1 as a satirical book, ignoring it's anomalous properties.

They don't realize that the original book is missing? They don't wonder where the new book came from?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did I profess my public love for this article? (Despite not thinking much of the last addendum)

Modified Author/Title: Bumberell, Peter. History on the couch.

Description of the modified book: The author describes prehistorical inventions in a jovial style, calling the man who first found fire and the man who invented the wheel as the "First protosociosophists".

This in particular is a nice conceptual shoutout to the Jara Cimrman mythos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Wittgenstein reference was absolutely glorious. I regret that I can only give one upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. That is actually a good point.

Changed to something more appropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only issue with this article is the use of Site 10 in the procedures. It's not a big deal, all things considered, but for some reason it broke my suspension of disbelief.

In terms of location IDs, 10 is not a large number for a global superpower; it probably indicates that this site is either long-standing or important in some way. Additionally, since the number is so low, it's quite possible that other articles have used it or will use it in the future. With all of that in mind, it seems odd that Site 10 is entirely paper media free. You'd think that there'd be a specialized site for that sort of thing, probably with a much longer or more specific designation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I'm changing to a neutral vote. The implementation is weak, but there's enough stuff I do like that I can't quite downvote it. The first paragraph of the last section ("I'd like to say hi…") is really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can only imagine that, in-universe, someone has made a version of buzzword bingo to help them get through reading SCP-2233-1 instances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great crezzy talk, I appreciated how almost-decipherable some of the samples were. +1

The last one (the Autolecture) was the least effective of the three for me; I don't know if it was too long, or too far down the word-salad/echolalia spectrum, or if I was just burnt out on the effort by that point - but I just had to start skimming the text. Then, I felt compelled to go back and re-read every word, lest I miss either an Easter Egg or a hook hidden in there. But there was no payoff. :( Perhaps that bit could be trimmed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It does get a little wordy towards the end, but I was okay with that. Then again, I really love Charles Dickens, so I'm typically okay with wordy, ha ha! I think for most people though, cutting out a few sentences here and there would be better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IK class end-of-the-world scenario "Information breakdown".

You actually used a non-standard K scenario. In a way that actually works. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's not much to that besides just a feel. Fifthism is something that resembles a religion but really isn't, and I got similar vibes from this article, so a similar direction might just work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. I started the article very excited about it, but by the end a lot of that excitement had been exhausted. Honestly, I think it could do without the -2 events entirely. At least as it is now, all that -2 material doesn't really add anything for me and seems to prevent you from exploring the potential of the central idea. Whatever you decide, I do hope you put some more work in because the potential is very intriguing and the parts I like, I like a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am glad you like it; I was on the fences with the entry too, with the examples being able to elaborate on their own. I might consider shortening the entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the comment: could you elaborate on your second paragraph? It might provide some good ideas for a next story on the topic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. My pet peeve was that it will be WAYYYY too long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the way you think. I'd like to expand on the concept further, so stay tuned!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to second this. It wasn't very impressive nonsense philosophy either. Try reading Derrida; this is a pale shadow. As Weizhong said, this used a lot of words to tell very little story, and to present relatively little that catches the imagination. It's not a bad concept, though I think the propagation is more dangerous than it needs to be, but the execution is weak. I do like the tone of the first paragraph of the last collapsible. It does have the ring of someone hopelessly opaque not being so.

EDIT: Instead of toning down the virulence, you could also turn it up. The "autolecture" instructions and the persons of interest materializing both have the feel of some appealing urban legend, and that direction could be explored further. Something that superficially resembles a philosophy is trying to birth itself into our world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmmmm… It's well written, but it feels too much like theta-prime to me. I'll come back to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was on board with the idea of this. It's a cool idea for philosophy to re-arrange itself into nonsensical meaning. It's intriguing, and kinda weird.

My main issue with this was the execution, in that not a lot was done with it. The last few logs all pretty much say the same thing; weird nonsensical philosophy. It became very repetitive very quickly, and also killed the mood for me. It may be personal taste, but I simply felt that it dragged on for far too long, in a manner that killed the impact of the article by dragging it on for far too long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this. As mentioned, it's like Star Signals, but the execution makes it unique. It's also very well written; descriptive but not overbearing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I adore this. It's kind of a sendup of all those times you were studying some erudite philosophical text in college and found yourself completely zoned out, looking at the words but comprehending none of them. A couple thoughts:

  • Describing reality-bending as "unknown" is kind of redundant.
  • I'd drop the full bibliography entry for Foucault's book. Change it to something like "a bibliographic entry for The History of Sexuality: Vol. 2 by Michel Foucault" to keep that sentence flowing. You can leave the other entry as-is, because it's giving us lots of great new information.

As an aside, all the examples given in the collapsible are great. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am so confused.
What I got from this is that -1 is media about a pseudo-scientific ideology called Sociosophy, and when put next to other forms of media, will turn them into other instances of -1s. When practitioners of Sociosophy come together and lecture about their topic in a special way, various reality-bending phenomena occur.
Now, do people who read -1 instances instantly and invariably change into PoIs, or do they just have to find the topic interesting? Does Sociosophy have any underlying theme to it, and is there any information as to what Sociosophists do/believe in that I missed from the article (in other words, what is Sociosophy?) Does this have any correlation to the Fifthist Church?
I +1'ed, though, because I do find the concept of having all of the world's knowledge replaced by some weird philosophy scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, I'm glad you do!

Jehoiada event is simply a designation of when the PoIs appear, so no mystery about that.

+ My headcanon of the procedure, which might vary for everyone.
- hide this content

Shortened description of a sophisticated raid with amnesiacs, hence the Lethe (memory) the Acheron(death) and Styx (both).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it.

It isn't Keter because it poses an innate threat to mankind or is hard to contain, it's dangerous because it threatens to fuck up all of academia through sesquipedalian, philosophical fabrications that anomalously transfer through vacuum in the event that it is located in close proximity to informational mediums.

I dislike the vagueness of Jehoida Events and Procedure Styx-Archeron, though.

Tentative +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It kinda reminded me of "Star Signals," but I liked it.
By the way, what kinda protocol takes its name from three of the rivers in hell?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Now give me my goddamn mustache back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: YAY!! It looks fabulous!! :-D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Credits:

Von Pincier, Fantem, Eldritch Manilo for thorough betareads.
Bryx, DenitaTwoDragons for betareads.
My real life schoolmate, who is a huge sociosophy buff himself, for the picture. :)
